refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to duty cycle and site conditions, not a functional requirement of the system itself. This separation allows DPs to address enablement (e.g., responsive controls, efficient hydraulics, predictable motion) independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and representative site conditions, the NGHE solution's subsystems (propulsion, actuation, control response, load handling) do not introduce artificial delays or constraints that prevent the cycle from completing within CT_target. Measure end-to-end cycle time in controlled trials; confirm that any shortfall vs. CT_target is attributable to operator technique, site conditions, or load variability—not system design limitations.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall execute the load phase (bucket fill, material consolidation) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall execute the travel phase (loaded and empty transit) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall execute the dump phase (material discharge, bucket return to load position) without introducing artificial time delays.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall provide responsive and predictable control inputs to the operator to enable efficient cycle execution.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors elapsed time against phase budgets and dynamically adjusts operational parameters (speed, acceleration, dwell) to meet p95 target without exceeding safety margins.

DP3: Design modular subsystem interfaces (payload handling, propulsion, positioning) such that each subsystem exposes cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (load time, travel time, dump time) enabling real-time feedback and adaptive sequencing.

DP4: Establish a duty-cycle characterization model (site conditions, payload mass distribution, terrain profile) that feeds into pre-cycle planning to set phase-time budgets and operational setpoints before execution begins.

DP5: Implement redundant or fail-safe timing and sequencing logic to ensure cycle integrity under sensor/actuator degradation, with graceful fallback to conservative (longer) cycle times rather than unsafe shortcuts.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP4 (integrated approach): Decompose the load→travel→dump cycle into independently optimizable phases with modular subsystem interfaces that expose phase-relevant metrics, informed by duty-cycle characterization. This avoids monolithic control logic (DP2 coupling risk) and ensures safety-first fallback (DP5 deferred to detailed design). The combination supports both real-time adaptation and pre-cycle planning.

potential_sources: Phase-gating logic must synchronize with subsystem readiness signals; loose coupling requires well-defined handshake interfaces (e.g., 'load complete' event) rather than time-based assumptions.
Duty-cycle model (DP4) must remain decoupled from real-time controller; if model parameters are hard-coded, site-condition changes force code redeployment (tight coupling risk).
Subsystem metrics (DP3) must be standardized across payload, propulsion, and positioning modules; inconsistent metric definitions or update rates will create hidden dependencies.
p95 acceptance criterion introduces statistical coupling: cycle-time variance must be characterized and controlled; if variance is high, p95 may exceed target even if mean is acceptable.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids naming specific control algorithms or hardware, maintaining solution neutrality. However, the mapping quality depends critically on interface clarity (phase events, metric definitions, parameter handoff) and on explicit variance management. Recommend explicit IM-layer design to isolate duty-cycle model, phase controller, and subsystem adapters.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cycle phases (load, travel, dump) are sequentially dependent and cannot overlap; if parallelization is possible (e.g., dump while returning), DP1 decomposition must be revisited.

ASSUMPTION: Subsystem performance (load time, travel time, dump time) is repeatable and measurable under the agreed duty cycle; if high variability exists (e.g., due to operator skill, terrain randomness), p95 target may be unachievable without active feedback control (DP2 elements may be necessary).

ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, payload distribution, obstacles) are known or can be characterized before cycle execution; if conditions are highly variable or unknown, pre-cycle planning (DP4) loses effectiveness.

ASSUMPTION: Safety plan and operational policies do not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization (e.g., mandatory dwell times, speed limits); if they do, cycle-time target may be infeasible.
RISK: p95 acceptance criterion requires statistical validation; if only mean cycle time is measured, acceptance may be false-positive. Recommend explicit p95 measurement protocol.
RISK: Subsystem interfaces may be poorly defined, leading to integration delays and hidden coupling during IM realization.
RISK: Duty-cycle model may be incomplete or inaccurate, causing pre-cycle budgets to be unrealistic.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly address: (1) Cycle-phase dependencies and parallelization constraints; (2) Acceptable variance and p95 measurement methodology; (3) Duty-cycle characterization scope (site conditions, payload ranges, operator skill levels); (4) Safety-critical constraints that may limit optimization. Propose IM-layer design with three modules: (A) Duty-Cycle Characterizer (owns DP4, inputs site/payload data, outputs phase-time budgets); (B) Phase Sequencer (owns DP1, enforces phase ordering, monitors phase-event handshakes); (C) Subsystem Adapter (owns DP3, translates phase budgets into subsystem setpoints, collects phase-time metrics). Define explicit interfaces: phase-event schema, metric definitions, parameter handoff protocol. Defer DP5 (redundancy/fallback) to detailed design after IM boundaries are locked.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates load→travel→dump cycle decomposition with phase-aware subsystem interfaces, duty-cycle metrics exposure, and modular control logic that avoids monolithic decision-making while deferring safety-critical fallback logic to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports phase-relevant metrics (load state, travel progress, dump completion, cycle timing) and duty-cycle characterization data to enable real-time adaptation and pre-cycle planning.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives cycle commands (load, travel, dump) and phase-sequencing directives from NGHE Solution or Teleoperation; supports independent phase optimization.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and fallback triggers from Safety Services; reports phase-level safety status and requests safety-first intervention when needed.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports teleoperation override and remote monitoring of cycle phases; enables low-latency phase state synchronization.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Vehicle naturally owns the load→travel→dump cycle decomposition, subsystem phase interfaces, and duty-cycle metrics. Phase-aware control logic is intrinsic to vehicle operation. Minor diffusion: safety-critical fallback (DP5) correctly deferred to Safety Services; teleoperation coordination with Teleoperation module is minimal and well-defined via Tasking/SafetyAlert.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a higher-level orchestration/planning layer. While it may consume phase metrics and issue cycle commands, it should not own the cycle decomposition logic or subsystem interfaces. Placing DP1+DP3+DP4 here would diffuse vehicle-specific phase control across the solution layer, violating separation of concerns and making vehicle reuse difficult.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. It should consume Telemetry and issue Tasking commands, not own cycle decomposition or phase metrics logic. Placing DP1+DP3+DP4 here would scatter vehicle control logic across the teleoperation module, creating tight coupling and poor modularity.

Very poor fit: Safety Services owns safety-critical fallback logic (DP5 deferred). Placing cycle decomposition and phase metrics here would conflate operational control with safety oversight, violating the safety-first principle and creating unnecessary coupling between nominal and safety-critical paths.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and most cohesive boundary for DP1+DP3+DP4 because: (1) it owns the physical/operational load→travel→dump cycle; (2) it can decompose this cycle into independently optimizable phases with clear subsystem interfaces; (3) it can expose phase-relevant metrics (load state, travel progress, dump completion, cycle timing) natively; (4) it can implement modular control logic that avoids monolithic decision-making by delegating phase sequencing to higher-level tasking (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) while retaining phase execution and metrics; (5) it maintains clean separation from safety-critical fallback (DP5), which belongs in Safety Services. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide well-defined boundaries for coordination without diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: If Vehicle module is not sufficiently decomposed internally, phase-aware subsystem interfaces (DP3) may become implicit or poorly documented, reducing modularity and reusability.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) requires real-time data collection and analysis; if Vehicle lacks adequate telemetry infrastructure, metrics may be incomplete or delayed, undermining real-time adaptation.

Risk: Tasking interface must be flexible enough to support both pre-cycle planning (NGHE Solution) and real-time teleoperation (Teleoperation); if Tasking is too rigid, phase independence (DP1) may be compromised.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface must provide low-latency safety-critical fallback triggers; if latency is high or interface is unreliable, safety-first principle (DP5 deferred) may be violated in practice.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is needed. DP1+DP3+DP4 is well-suited to Vehicle containment. However, recommend explicit architectural refinement: (1) Define phase-aware subsystem interfaces (DP3) as formal contracts (e.g., load subsystem interface, travel subsystem interface, dump subsystem interface) with clear input/output metrics; (2) Implement duty-cycle characterization (DP4) as a dedicated Vehicle sub-module (e.g., 'Cycle Analyzer') that collects and exposes phase metrics via Telemetry; (3) Ensure Tasking interface supports both command-driven (pre-cycle planning) and event-driven (real-time adaptation) phase sequencing; (4) Validate SafetyAlert latency and reliability requirements to ensure DP5 fallback can be triggered safely from Safety Services.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Vehicle module's phase-aware decomposition (load→travel→dump) with exposed metrics (load state, travel progress, dump completion, cycle timing) directly enables measurement of p95 cycle time against CT_target. Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) via Telemetry interface provides real-time data collection under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. SafetyAlert interface ensures worksite safety plan compliance through safety-critical fallback delegation to Safety Services.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR allocation to Vehicle is unambiguous: Vehicle owns cycle execution and metrics exposure. Tasking interface receives cycle commands from higher-level orchestration (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation). SafetyAlert interface receives safety constraints from Safety Services. This separation ensures cycle time measurement is decoupled from safety-critical logic, reducing verification complexity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1+DP3+DP4 (phase decomposition + phase-aware subsystem interfaces + duty-cycle characterization) directly addresses FR requirement to enable cycle execution within CT_target. Phase independence (DP1) supports real-time adaptation and pre-cycle planning. Phase-aware interfaces (DP3) expose metrics needed for cycle time measurement. Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) grounds optimization in actual worksite conditions. Deferral of safety-critical fallback (DP5) to Safety Services avoids monolithic control logic and maintains safety-first principle. DP choice is sound and well-justified.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Phase-aware subsystem interfaces (DP3) must be formally documented as contracts (load subsystem interface, travel subsystem interface, dump subsystem interface) with explicit input/output metrics. If interfaces remain implicit or poorly specified, phase independence (DP1) will degrade and reusability will suffer. Mitigation: Define interface specifications in detailed design with clear ownership and versioning.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) requires continuous real-time data collection and analysis. If Vehicle module lacks dedicated telemetry infrastructure (e.g., 'Cycle Analyzer' sub-module), metrics may be scattered across subsystems, creating diffusion and latency issues. Mitigation: Implement DP4 as a dedicated Vehicle sub-module with clear responsibility for metrics aggregation and Telemetry export.

Risk: Tasking interface must support both command-driven (pre-cycle planning from NGHE Solution) and event-driven (real-time adaptation from Teleoperation) phase sequencing. If Tasking is too rigid or monolithic, phase independence (DP1) may be compromised and real-time adaptation capability will be lost. Mitigation: Design Tasking interface with explicit support for both command and event modes; validate in integration testing.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface must provide low-latency, reliable safety-critical fallback triggers from Safety Services. If latency is high or interface is unreliable, DP5 (safety-first fallback) cannot be guaranteed in practice, violating safety-first principle. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert latency and reliability requirements in detailed design; validate with safety analysis (FMEA, FTA).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment fit (0.92 score). Vehicle module naturally owns load→travel→dump cycle decomposition, phase-aware subsystem interfaces, and duty-cycle metrics. Phase-aware control logic is intrinsic to vehicle operation. Minor diffusion risks are well-managed: safety-critical fallback (DP5) is correctly deferred to Safety Services; teleoperation coordination is minimal and well-defined via Tasking/SafetyAlert interfaces. No alternative module (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) is suitable for DP1+DP3+DP4 ownership. Recommended refinement: (1) Formalize phase-aware subsystem interfaces as explicit contracts; (2) Implement duty-cycle characterization as dedicated Vehicle sub-module; (3) Validate Tasking interface flexibility for both command and event modes; (4) Establish and validate SafetyAlert latency/reliability requirements.

recommended_revisions: Formalize phase-aware subsystem interfaces (DP3) as explicit architectural contracts in detailed design. Define load subsystem interface, travel subsystem interface, and dump subsystem interface with clear input/output metrics, timing requirements, and error handling. Document interface ownership and versioning to ensure modularity and reusability.

Implement duty-cycle characterization (DP4) as a dedicated Vehicle sub-module (e.g., 'Cycle Analyzer') responsible for collecting, aggregating, and exposing phase-relevant metrics (load state, travel progress, dump completion, cycle timing) via Telemetry interface. This centralizes DP4 logic and reduces diffusion risk.

Design Tasking interface to support both command-driven (pre-cycle planning) and event-driven (real-time adaptation) phase sequencing. Validate interface flexibility in integration testing to ensure phase independence (DP1) is preserved under both operational modes.

Establish explicit SafetyAlert latency and reliability requirements in detailed design (e.g., max latency for safety-critical fallback trigger, minimum availability/MTBF). Validate requirements through safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) and integration testing to ensure DP5 (safety-first fallback) can be guaranteed in practice.

Define acceptance test plan for cycle time measurement (p95 ≤ CT_target) that exercises Vehicle module under agreed duty cycle and site conditions. Ensure Telemetry interface provides sufficient granularity for phase-level cycle time analysis and root-cause investigation of any p95 exceedances.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The Vehicle module's phase-aware decomposition (load→travel→dump) is sound, interfaces are well-defined, and containment is excellent (0.92 score). However, the recommended revisions—particularly formalizing phase-aware subsystem interfaces (DP3) as explicit contracts and implementing duty-cycle characterization (DP4) as a dedicated sub-module—require next-level decomposition to operationalize. The high safety criticality of the system and the explicit diffusion risks around SafetyAlert latency/reliability further justify drilling down to ensure safety-critical requirements are properly allocated and verified at lower levels. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition will clarify interface contracts, safety-critical fallback mechanisms, and telemetry architecture before detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Enable phase-independent load subsystem operation with explicit input/output interface contract (load command, load state, load completion signal, error handling).
FR1.2: Enable phase-independent travel subsystem operation with explicit input/output interface contract (travel command, travel progress, travel completion signal, error handling).
FR1.3: Enable phase-independent dump subsystem operation with explicit input/output interface contract (dump command, dump state, dump completion signal, error handling).
FR2.1: Aggregate and expose phase-relevant cycle metrics (load state, travel progress, dump completion, cycle timing, phase duration) via Telemetry interface with defined granularity and latency.

FR2.2: Characterize actual duty cycle (load mass distribution, travel distance/terrain, dump site conditions, cycle frequency) from real-time telemetry data and enable duty-cycle-aware cycle time optimization.
FR3.1: Support command-driven phase sequencing (pre-cycle planning from NGHE Solution) with explicit task queue and phase state machine.
FR3.2: Support event-driven phase sequencing (real-time adaptation from Teleoperation) with explicit event handlers and phase transition logic.
FR4.1: Receive and enforce safety-critical fallback triggers from Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface with guaranteed low-latency, high-reliability delivery.
FR4.2: Implement safety-first fallback behavior (e.g., emergency stop, safe state transition) upon SafetyAlert trigger with explicit state machine and error recovery logic.

revise.why: Recommended revisions from gate analysis require explicit DP refinement at next level: (1) DP3 (phase-aware subsystem interfaces) must be decomposed into three explicit interface contracts (load, travel, dump) with formal specifications; (2) DP4 (duty-cycle characterization) must be decomposed into a dedicated metrics aggregation sub-module with clear ownership and telemetry export logic; (3) DP2 (Tasking interface) must be decomposed into command-driven and event-driven sequencing modes with explicit state machine; (4) DP5 (SafetyAlert interface) must be decomposed into latency-critical fallback trigger reception and safety-first state transition logic. These refinements operationalize the high-level DP choices and reduce diffusion risk.

assumptions: Phase independence (DP1) is achievable through explicit interface contracts and state machine design; subsystems can operate autonomously within phase boundaries without tight coupling.

Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) can be implemented as a dedicated Vehicle sub-module without significant performance overhead; telemetry data collection and aggregation do not introduce unacceptable latency.
Tasking interface can support both command-driven and event-driven sequencing modes without architectural conflict; mode switching is deterministic and well-defined.
SafetyAlert interface can achieve required latency and reliability through dedicated communication channel and priority-based message handling; safety-critical fallback is guaranteed by design.

Phase-aware subsystem interfaces (load, travel, dump) can be formally specified with clear input/output contracts, timing requirements, and error handling; interface ownership is unambiguous and versioning is manageable.
open_risks: Risk: Phase-aware subsystem interfaces (DP3) may degrade if not formally documented as explicit contracts. Mitigation: Decompose FR1.1, FR1.2, FR1.3 to define interface specifications with clear ownership and versioning.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) may suffer from metric diffusion if not implemented as dedicated sub-module. Mitigation: Decompose FR2.1, FR2.2 to implement Cycle Analyzer sub-module with centralized metrics aggregation.

Risk: Tasking interface may be too rigid for real-time adaptation if command-driven and event-driven modes are not explicitly supported. Mitigation: Decompose FR3.1, FR3.2 to design dual-mode sequencing with explicit state machine.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may not meet latency/reliability requirements if not explicitly designed for safety-critical operation. Mitigation: Decompose FR4.1, FR4.2 to establish latency/reliability requirements and validate through safety analysis (FMEA, FTA).

Risk: High safety criticality requires explicit safety-critical fallback logic; if DP5 is not properly decomposed, safety-first principle may be compromised. Mitigation: Decompose FR4.1, FR4.2 to implement safety-critical state machine with explicit error recovery.
evidence_needed: Formal interface specifications for load, travel, dump subsystems (FR1.1, FR1.2, FR1.3) with input/output contracts, timing requirements, error handling, and ownership documentation.
Cycle Analyzer sub-module design (FR2.1, FR2.2) with metrics aggregation logic, telemetry export interface, and duty-cycle characterization algorithm.
Tasking interface design (FR3.1, FR3.2) with explicit support for command-driven and event-driven sequencing modes; state machine diagram and mode-switching logic.
SafetyAlert interface design (FR4.1, FR4.2) with latency/reliability requirements, priority-based message handling, and safety-critical fallback state machine.
Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) for SafetyAlert interface and safety-critical fallback logic to validate that DP5 (safety-first fallback) can be guaranteed in practice.
Integration test plan for phase-aware subsystem interfaces and Tasking interface flexibility to validate phase independence (DP1) under both command-driven and event-driven operational modes.
Acceptance test plan for cycle time measurement (p95 ≤ CT_target) with telemetry data collection and phase-level cycle time analysis under agreed duty cycle and site conditions.
